metadata.title: A Short Introduction to Newtonian Physics
metadata.author: Engineering Lecture Notes
metadata.description: An introductory presentation covering Newtonian physics: Newton's laws, force, energy, work, power, pressure, flow, and the hydraulic analogy applied to electronics.

content.text: Title slide introducing the presentation topic and scope
A Short Introduction to Newtonian Physics
Energy, Work, Power, Force, Pressure and Flow
v1.10 — June 2022 | Prerequisite Knowledge: None
Introduction slide explaining the motivation and scope of the chapter
1.1 Introduction
Electronics — and all engineering — is fundamentally applied physics. This chapter introduces the most important principles in Newtonian physics essential to understanding electronic circuits.
Topics covered: energy, work, power, force, pressure, and flow — explored through the hydraulic analogy (water in pipes), then mapped to electronics.
Note: Gravity is neglected throughout. Assume all pipes lie on a flat horizontal surface.
Newton's three laws of motion, leading to F = ma
1.2 Newton's Laws of Motion
1. A body remains at rest or in uniform motion unless acted on by an external force.
2. The rate of change of momentum is equal to the net force applied.
3. For every action there is an equal and opposite reaction.
From Law 2, since mass is usually constant: F = ma
Force, energy, and work definitions with examples
1.3 Force, Energy and Work
Force (F) — measured in newtons (N). 1 N accelerates 1 kg at 1 m/s².
Energy (E) — the ability to do work, measured in joules (J). 1 J = 1 N·m
Work (W) — applying force over a distance: W = F·d
Power (P) — work per unit time: P = W/t (measured in watts, W)
Pressure and flow rate with hydraulic analogy
1.4 Pressure and Flow
Pressure (P) — force per unit area. In hydraulic systems, pressure drives flow through resistance.
Flow rate (Q) — volume per unit time (cubic metres per second or litres per minute).
Hydraulic Analogy: Pressure ~ Voltage, Flow ~ Current, Resistance ~ Component Resistance
Energy conservation principle and power dissipation in resistors
1.5 Conservation of Energy
Energy is neither created nor destroyed — it is converted from one form to another.
In a circuit, energy supplied by the battery is dissipated as heat in resistors: P = V·I = I²·R = V²/R
Heat generated in a resistor over time t: Q = I²·R·t (energy dissipated in joules)
Complete mapping of hydraulic analogy to electrical circuits
1.6 The Hydraulic Analogy
Water in pipes → Electrons in wires
Pump pressure (Pa) → Battery voltage (V)
Water flow rate (m³/s) → Current (A)
Pipe narrowness → Resistance (Ω)
Power dissipated in pump: P = Pressure × Flow = V × I
Summary of Newtonian physics key concepts
Summary — Newtonian Physics
• Force (F) and acceleration: F = ma
• Work and power: W = F·d, P = W/t
• Energy conservation: total energy is constant in a closed system
• Pressure and flow: analogous to voltage and current in circuits
• Power dissipation: P = V·I = I²·R = V²/R
